9: ou bradynei Kyrios tes epangelias hos tines bradyteta hegountai alla makrothymei eis hymas me boulomenos tinas apolesai alla pantas eis metanoian chorein (ou bradynei Kyrios tēs epangelias, hōs tines bradytēta hēgountai, alla makrothymei eis hymas, mē boulomenos tinas apolesthai alla pantas eis metanoian chōrein): 'The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish but that all should reach repentance.' Makrothymia (patience/longsuffering) reframes the delayed parousia as a divine mercy-extension rather than a failed prediction. The universalistic-sounding 'not wishing that any should perish' is balanced by 'toward you' — the patience is specifically exercised toward the addressees, inviting them toward repentance. The passage is one of the key texts on divine patience and the purpose of eschatological delay.
1: eilikrinē dianoia — pure/sincere understanding (eilikrinēs = tested by the sun, genuinely pure; the etymology connects to examining something in direct sunlight to detect adulteration — applied to the mind that has been examined and found genuine). en hypomnēsei diegeirein — to stir up by way of reminder (hypomnēsis = a bringing to mind; diegeirō = to arouse completely — the same word as 2 Peter 1:13).
2: mnēsthēnai tōn proeirēmenōn rhēmatōn hypo tōn hagiōn prophētōn — to remember the words previously spoken by the holy prophets (proeirēmenōn = having been spoken before — the OT prophets speak in advance of fulfillment). kai tēs tōn apostolōn hymōn entolēs tou Kyriou — and the commandment of the Lord given through your apostles (the two authoritative sources: prophets and apostles; the letter grounds its appeal in this dual witness structure).
3: en eschate tōn hēmerōn empaiktai en empaigmonē — in the last days mockers will come, scoffing (empaiktēs = scoffer, mocker — a hapax in the NT but appearing also in Jude 18; the compound: en + paizō = to play as a child, to mock). kata tas idias autōn epithymias poreuomenoi — walking according to their own desires (the scoffers' eschatological denial is rooted in their desire to live without accountability).
4: pou estin hē epaggelia tēs parousias autou — where is the promise of his coming? (epaggelia = promise; the scoffers challenge the promise on empirical grounds: no coming has occurred). panta houtōs diamenei — all things continue as they were (diamenō = to continue through without change; ap'archēs ktiseōs — from the beginning of creation; the argument is from the uniformity of the natural order — a form of proto-uniformitarianism that denies divine intervention).
5: touto gar autois thelontas lanthanei — for this escapes them, willing it to escape (lanthanō = to be hidden, to escape notice; thelontas = willing/choosing — the ignorance is chosen, not accidental; the scoffers want not to know). ek logou Theou ouranoi ēsan ekpalai — by the word of God heavens existed long ago (logos Theou = the creative word of Gen 1; the same word that sustains creation can also judge it). gē ex hydatos kai di'hydatos synesōsa — the earth having been formed out of water and through water.
6: di'hōn ho tote kosmos hydati kataklysmenos apōleto — through which the then-world being deluged with water was destroyed (kataklysmenos = flooded — the word that gives us "cataclysm"; the flood is the historical precedent that refutes uniformitarianism: the world-as-it-was did not continue; it was destroyed by water). The scoffers' uniformitarian argument is built on deliberate amnesia of the flood.
7: hoi de nyn ouranoi kai hē gē tō autō logō tethēsaurismenoi eisin — but the present heavens and earth by the same word have been stored up/treasured (thēsaurizō = to store as treasure — the cosmic order is not permanent but stored for future use; logō = by the word — the same divine creative word of 2 Peter 3:5). pyri tēroumenoi eis hēmeran kriseōs — being guarded by fire for the day of judgment (a chiasm with the flood: the first world was destroyed by water; the present world is guarded by/for fire).
8: hen de touto mē lanthanetō hymas — but do not let this one thing escape your notice (echoing 2 Peter 3:5's lanthanei — the scoffers chose not to know; Peter's readers must actively prevent this from escaping their notice). hoti mia hēmera para Kyriō hōs chilia etē kai chilia etē hōs hēmera mia — that one day with the Lord is as a thousand years and a thousand years as one day (Psalms 90:4 LXX citation/allusion: Moses' prayer acknowledging that from God's perspective, a thousand years are like yesterday when past — the divine relationship to time utterly differs from human time-experience; the scoffers' argument from temporal delay collapses against divine trans-temporality).
10: hēxei de hēmera Kyriou hōs kleptēs — but the day of the Lord will come like a thief (the thief-image for the Day of the Lord: unexpected arrival that cannot be prepared for by the unprepared; cf. Matthew 24:43; 1 Thessalonians 5:2; Revelation 3:3; 2 Peter 16:15). stoicheia de kausoumena lythēsetai — the elements (stoicheia = basic constituents; here likely the heavenly bodies or the fundamental components of the cosmos) being burned will be dissolved (lyō = to loose, dissolve). kai gē kai ta en autē erga katakahēsetai — and the earth and the works in it will be found/burned (heurethēsetai or katakahēsetai — the text is disputed; either "will be found out" = exposed, or "will be burned up").
11: toutōn houtōs pantōn lyomenōn — these things being thus dissolved (present passive participle — the dissolution is the assumed condition for the moral argument that follows). potapous dei hyparchein hymas — what sort of persons ought you to be? (potapos = of what sort, from what country/origin — the question about the quality of character that the eschatological perspective requires). en hagiais anastrophais kai eusebeiais — in holy conduct/ways of life and acts of godliness (plural anastrophais and eusebeiais — the multiple concrete instances of the holy life).
12: prosdokōntas kai speudontas tēn parousian tēs tou Theou hēmeras — expecting and hastening the coming/presence of the day of God (speudontas = hastening, causing to hasten — the startling claim that human conduct can hasten the Day; Jewish tradition held that Israel's repentance would hasten the messianic age; here, holy conduct and godliness participate in the divine timing). di'hēn ouranoi pyroumenoi lythēsontai — on account of which the heavens being set on fire will be dissolved.
13: kainous de ouranous kai kainēn gēn kata to epaggeltna autou prosdokōmen — but new heavens and a new earth according to his promise we expect (kainous = new in kind/quality, not just new in time — the kainos vs. neos distinction; the new heavens and earth are qualitatively different, not merely temporally subsequent). The promise cites Isaiah 65:17; 2 Peter 66:22. en hois dikaiosynē katoikei — in which righteousness dwells (katoikei = to dwell as a permanent resident; the new order has righteousness as its permanent inhabitant — contrasting with the false teachers who inhabit the present order).
14: prosdokōntes tauta spoudasate amōmētoi kai aspiloi autō heurethēnai — eagerly expecting these things, make every effort to be found blameless and spotless before him (amōmētos = without censure, blameless; aspilos = without spot/stain — the same purity terms as 1 Peter 1:19's lamb without defect; the eschatological expectation produces the moral urgency to be found worthy). en eirēnē — in peace; the peace that characterizes the eschatological community is already to be the present condition of those expecting it.
15: tēn tou Kyriou hēmōn makrothymian sōtērian hēgeisthe — consider the patience of our Lord as salvation (makrothymia = long-suffering patience, the capacity to wait without retaliation; God's apparent delay in the Day of the Lord is reframed as salvific opportunity rather than divine inattention). kathōs kai ho agapētos hēmōn adelphos Paulos — as also our beloved brother Paul (the only NT text outside the Pauline letters that cites Paul as an authoritative author; the designation agapētos adelphos — beloved brother — establishes collegial apostolic equality).
16: hōs kai en pasais tais epistolais lalōn en autais peri toutōn — as also in all his letters, speaking in them concerning these things (the reference to "all his letters" implies a recognized collection). tas loipas graphas — the rest of the Scriptures (graphas = the Scriptures — the word used for authoritative scriptural writings; placing Paul's letters alongside "the rest of the Scriptures" is a significant statement about early canonization). dysnoēta — hard to understand (dysnoētos = difficult to comprehend — an honest acknowledgment).
17: phylassesthe hina mē tē tōn athesmōn planē synapachthentes — guard yourselves lest, being swept along by the error of the lawless (synapagō = to carry away together with, to be swept along by), ekpesēte tou idiou stērigmou — you fall from your own stability (stērigmos = stability, foundation — the noun of stērizō = to establish; the established stability described in 1:12 is the thing at risk; the letter's warning circles back to its opening concern).
18: auxanete de en chariti kai gnōsei tou Kyriou hēmōn kai sōtēros Iēsou Christou — grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ (the concluding exhortation returns to the letter's opening theme: knowledge — gnōsis here rather than the letter's usual epignōsis, but the same semantic range; growth in grace and knowledge together). autō hē doxa kai nyn kai eis hēmeran aiōnos — to him the glory both now and to the day of eternity (the doxology closes the letter; hēmera aiōnos = the day of eternity — a unique phrase; eternity as a day that never ends).
